summary: engine/wal/writer.go adds Writer/OpenWriter/Append/Close/ReadSegment, the first real code in what was previously a scaffold-only engine/wal/ package (bare doc.go), establishing the on-disk record framing and segment-rotation convention that subtasks 1.3.2-1.3.5 will build on. Verified independently against issue #3's literal checklist item 1.3.1 (fetched via `gh issue view 3`, per CDR isolation I4 -- no access to implementer's plan/reasoning): acceptance criteria text ('Log records append to wal/wal-<segment>.log; a new segment is created once the current one exceeds a configured size, with no record ever split across segments') and test spec ('go test ./engine/wal/... -run TestSegmentWriter -race') match the implementer's claim verbatim. Read writer.go and writer_test.go in full. Record format is an 8-byte header (uint32 LE payload length at offset 0, uint32 LE CRC32-IEEE of payload at offset 4) followed by raw payload; ReadSegment parses this back, verifying header/payload truncation AND CRC match, so both write and read/parse sides exist in this subtask (not write-only) -- this parser is what makes TestSegmentWriter's 'record integrity' and 'no split across segments' assertions genuinely empirical rather than asserted-by-fiat. Traced the rotation conditional directly: `if w.size > 0 && w.size+total > w.maxSegmentBytes { rotate }` uses strict '>', so a record that lands EXACTLY at maxSegmentBytes stays in the current segment (matches the acceptance criterion's 'exceeds', not 'reaches' semantics) -- confirmed this is deliberate via the total>maxSegmentBytes hard-error check earlier in Append using the same strict operator, not an accidental off-by-one. Rotation itself happens strictly before the write (rotateLocked() is called and returns before any header/payload bytes are written for the new record), so no record is ever partially written into an about-to-overflow segment. TestAppendOversizedRecordHardErrors confirms a record whose own header+payload exceeds maxSegmentBytes is hard-rejected before any bytes are written (verified by ReadSegment afterward showing the segment contains only the subsequent small record, not a partial/corrupt oversized one). TestOpenWriterResumesExistingSegments confirms OpenWriter picks the correct highest-N segment, reopens it with O_APPEND (so subsequent Write calls append rather than overwrite), and a resumed writer's second Append lands after the first writer's existing record without clobbering it -- read via ReadSegment showing both records present and in order. This resume path deliberately does NOT validate the resumed segment's tail for torn/partial records (explicitly documented in both OpenWriter's doc comment and the commit message as deferred to 1.3.4/1.3.5); this is a reasonable, clearly-flagged deferral given a hard file-format decision (torn-tail detection needs the record-type/replay layer that doesn't exist yet), not a silent landmine -- but it does mean that if a real crash left a torn tail in segment N and the process is restarted today (before 1.3.4/1.3.5 land), OpenWriter will blindly O_APPEND new records after the torn bytes, producing a segment ReadSegment cannot parse from that point forward; this is called out below as a non-blocking finding since it is explicitly in-scope-deferred, not a regression introduced by this diff. Append genuinely calls file.Sync() after every Write, before returning success, matching the WAL durability requirement and the repo's Sync-before-return idiom (used sequentially, not WriteAt, since this is a genuinely append-only log rather than the fixed-offset-record idiom used by engine/catalog/idalloc.go and engine/btree/node.go's hard-error-on-overflow convention, which IS matched exactly for the oversized-record case). Ran go build ./engine/..., go vet ./engine/..., go test ./engine/wal/... -run TestSegmentWriter -race -v, the full ./engine/wal/... suite, and the full ./engine/... suite (btree, catalog, wal) with -race -count=1: all pass, no regressions.

requirements.evidence: Fetched issue #3's checklist item 1.3.1 directly via `gh issue view 3` and confirmed the acceptance criteria and test spec text match the implementer's claim verbatim: segments named wal-<N>.log within the caller's dir, rotation on exceeding configured size, no record split across segments, verified by `go test ./engine/wal/... -run TestSegmentWriter -race`. TestSegmentWriter exists with exactly this name and does force >=2 rotations (60 records, maxSegmentBytes=200, asserting >=3 segment files created) and asserts both segment-size boundaries and full record-integrity round-trip. Impacted modules match exactly: engine/wal/writer.go, engine/wal/writer_test.go.

architecture_conformance.evidence: Length-prefixed, little-endian, hard-error-not-truncate-on-overflow framing genuinely mirrors engine/btree/node.go's encode() functions (confirmed by reading node.go lines ~110-155: identical 'return explicit error rather than silently truncate/exceed NodeSize' pattern, just applied to a variable-size record instead of a fixed-size node page). Sync-before-return durability discipline genuinely mirrors engine/catalog/idalloc.go's Next() and file.go's WritePage (confirmed by reading both: WriteAt+Sync there, Write+Sync here). One overstatement in the commit message/doc comments: the commit claims the durability idiom is 'WriteAt+Sync' matching catalog, but writer.go actually uses plain sequential file.Write (not WriteAt) for appends -- a reasonable and arguably more idiomatic choice for a genuinely append-only log (WriteAt would require the caller to track/pass an explicit offset, which is unnecessary here since Append always writes at the current end-of-file under a single mutex), but the doc comment's phrasing overclaims idiom-matching precision. Not a functional bug, just an imprecise claim worth tightening in a follow-up doc pass.

regression_risk.evidence: Diff is entirely additive: two new files in a previously scaffold-only package (engine/wal/writer.go, engine/wal/writer_test.go), zero changes to engine/btree or engine/catalog. Ran the full engine/... test suite with -race -count=1: engine/btree, engine/catalog, and engine/wal all pass together, confirming no cross-package regression.

edge_cases.evidence: Exactly-at-limit boundary (record total bytes landing exactly at maxSegmentBytes) is handled correctly via strict '>' comparison in both the rotation check and the oversized-record hard-error check, confirmed by direct code reading -- this is deliberate (both checks use the same strict operator consistently) but is NOT covered by an explicit dedicated test case (TestSegmentWriter's 200-byte segments with ~28-30 byte records don't deterministically hit an exact-boundary record). Oversized single-record hard-error is tested and confirmed to leave no partial/corrupt data on disk (ReadSegment afterward shows only the subsequent valid record). Resume-from-existing-segments correctly picks the highest-N segment and does not clobber existing data (tested), but does not validate the resumed segment's tail for torn records before blindly O_APPEND-ing further writes after it -- explicitly and reasonably deferred to 1.3.4/1.3.5 per the doc comment and commit message, but this means a real mid-write crash today (before those subtasks land) would leave a segment that ReadSegment cannot parse past the torn point, with no protection yet. This is in-scope-deferred, not a newly introduced landmine, but should be tracked so 1.3.4/1.3.5 do not silently skip it.

security.evidence: No new external/untrusted input surface: dir is caller-supplied (already-trusted internal engine wiring), maxSegmentBytes is caller-supplied config, payload bytes are opaque caller data with no parsing/deserialization performed by this subtask (record *type* semantics deferred to 1.3.2). File permissions (0o644 for segments, 0o755 for MkdirAll) match the repo's existing conventions in catalog. CRC32 is used for integrity detection, not as a security/tamper-resistance mechanism, which is appropriate for its stated purpose (torn-record detection, not adversarial tamper detection).

performance.evidence: Single mutex serializes Append (matching IDAllocator's narrow single-purpose locking style, justified since every call must do synchronous durable I/O regardless), which is a correct and simple choice at this layer; no unnecessary allocations beyond the fixed 8-byte header buffer per call. fsync-per-Append is the correct, required durability tradeoff for a WAL and is explicitly the acceptance-criteria-driven design, not an oversight.

maintainability.evidence: Doc comments in writer.go are thorough and explicitly cross-reference which future subtasks (1.3.2/1.3.4/1.3.5) depend on which decisions made here, which is valuable given this is a brand-new package establishing conventions. However docs/LLD/wal.md remains scaffold-level ('Status: scaffold only') and was not updated by this diff to reflect the concrete record format (8-byte CRC32 header), segment-naming convention, or the deferred-torn-tail-validation decision -- consistent with the same LLD-staleness pattern already flagged in prior 1.2.x verification runs for engine/btree, so this is an inherited process gap rather than something newly introduced by this specific diff, but is worth resolving before 1.3.2 builds further on this foundation given wal.md is the only pre-existing design document for this package.

test_coverage.evidence: TestSegmentWriter genuinely empirically verifies the core acceptance criterion: it reads back every segment file (not just the last) via ReadSegment, confirms each segment's own bytes parse cleanly end-to-end (a split/torn record would surface as a truncation/CRC error here), asserts every segment stays <= maxSegmentBytes, and asserts full-fidelity, in-order byte equality between all 60 originally-appended records and all parsed-back records across all segments concatenated -- this is a strong, non-weak proxy for 'no record ever split across segments', not a count-only or size-only check. TestAppendOversizedRecordHardErrors and TestOpenWriterResumesExistingSegments cover their named scenarios adequately. Gaps: no dedicated test for the exact-boundary case (record total bytes landing exactly at maxSegmentBytes staying in the current segment rather than triggering rotation), and no test for reopening/resuming with more than one existing segment file (only ever tested with exactly one pre-existing segment) or for a corrupted/torn resumed segment (deferred by design, per above).

confidence.evidence: Directly verified issue #3's checklist text via `gh issue view 3` (not relying on the implementer's paraphrase). Read writer.go and writer_test.go in full, traced the rotation and oversized-record conditionals line by line, cross-referenced the claimed idiom-matching against engine/btree/node.go and engine/catalog/idalloc.go by reading both directly, and independently ran go build/go vet/go test -race -count=1 for engine/wal alone and the full engine/... suite, all passing. Confirmed HEAD contains the reviewed commit as an ancestor and that .cdr/index/task.jsonl's task-1.3.1 entry records the exact same commit SHA under review, with no correction needed.

explicit_determinations.record_format_roundtrip_and_reader_exist: Confirmed: an 8-byte header (uint32 LE length + uint32 LE CRC32-IEEE) precedes the raw payload. ReadSegment is a genuine, full reader/parser (not merely a claimed-for-later stub) that detects truncated headers, truncated payloads, and CRC mismatches, and is exercised directly by TestSegmentWriter to assert integrity -- so this subtask implements both write AND read/parse sides, and 'record integrity'/'no split across segments' in the test are verified empirically via this real parser, not merely asserted.
explicit_determinations.rotation_before_overflow_boundary: Confirmed via direct code trace: `if w.size > 0 && w.size+total > w.maxSegmentBytes { rotateLocked() }` uses strict greater-than, executed and returned BEFORE any header/payload bytes are written for the new record. A record landing EXACTLY at maxSegmentBytes (w.size+total == maxSegmentBytes) does NOT trigger rotation and stays in the current segment, correctly matching the acceptance criterion's 'exceeds' (not 'reaches') wording. This is deliberate, consistent with the also-strict '>' used in the earlier total>maxSegmentBytes hard-error check, not an accidental off-by-one -- though no dedicated test exercises this exact boundary value.
explicit_determinations.oversized_single_record: Confirmed: `if total > w.maxSegmentBytes { return error }` fires before any bytes are written, for a record whose own header+payload can never fit even in an empty segment. TestAppendOversizedRecordHardErrors verifies both the error and, via a subsequent ReadSegment call, that the segment contains only the later valid record -- no partial/corrupt bytes from the rejected oversized record were left on disk.
explicit_determinations.no_split_across_segments_empirically_tes: Confirmed as genuinely empirical, not a weak count-only proxy: TestSegmentWriter forces >=3 segments (60 records at ~28-30 bytes each into 200-byte segments), calls ReadSegment on EVERY segment file (ReadSegment fails hard on any truncated header/payload or CRC mismatch, which is exactly what a split record would produce), concatenates all parsed records across all segments in file order, and asserts full byte-for-byte, in-order equality against all 60 originally-appended payloads. This is the strongest form of this test short of literal byte-level file inspection, and it holds up.
explicit_determinations.fsync_before_return: Confirmed: Append calls w.file.Write(header) then optionally w.file.Write(payload) then w.file.Sync(), returning the error from Sync if it fails, and only returning success after Sync completes -- genuine fsync-before-return, not merely Write-before-return.
explicit_determinations.resume_from_existing_segments: Confirmed correct for its stated (deliberately narrow) scope: latestSegmentNum scans the directory, correctly identifies the highest-numbered wal-<N>.log file, and OpenWriter reopens it with O_APPEND so subsequent writes cannot clobber existing bytes; TestOpenWriterResumesExistingSegments confirms both writers' records coexist correctly in order. However, resume does NOT validate the resumed segment's tail for torn/partial records before appending further data -- this is explicitly documented as deferred to 1.3.4/1.3.5 in both the doc comment and commit message, which is a reasonable and clearly-flagged deferral rather than a silent landmine, but it does mean a real crash-then-restart today (before those subtasks land) would leave a segment ReadSegment cannot cleanly parse past the torn point.
explicit_determinations.idiom_matching_writeat_vs_write: Partial overstatement found: the commit message and some doc comments claim writer.go's durability discipline matches catalog's 'WriteAt+Sync' idiom, but writer.go actually uses plain sequential file.Write (not WriteAt) for appends. This is a reasonable design choice for a genuinely append-only log (no explicit offset tracking needed under the single mutex), not a functional bug, but the claim of idiom-matching precision is imprecise and should be tightened in a follow-up doc pass rather than left implying a literal WriteAt call exists.
explicit_determinations.issue_text_verification: Confirmed via `gh issue view 3`: the 1.3.1 checklist item's acceptance criteria, test spec, and impacted modules text match the implementer's claim verbatim.
explicit_determinations.self_referential_sha_check: .cdr/index/task.jsonl's task-1.3.1 entry records commit 1a12643cfadd40610afa690d32672e6c59928870, matching the commit under review exactly; confirmed via `git merge-base --is-ancestor` that this commit is an ancestor of the current HEAD (bed1e483b42a6b42ce2e39ad62c6a37286c99468).

commands_run: go test ./engine/... -race -count=1 (btree, catalog, wal all pass together, no regressions)

non_blocking_findings.summary: OpenWriter's resume path does not validate the resumed segment's tail for a torn/partial record before appending further writes; if the process crashed mid-write in a prior run leaving a torn tail, a restart today (before 1.3.4/1.3.5 land) would blindly O_APPEND new valid records after the torn bytes, producing a segment ReadSegment can no longer parse past the torn point. This is explicitly documented as deferred to 1.3.4/1.3.5 in both the doc comment and commit message, so it is an intentional, flagged scope boundary rather than an oversight, but it is a real gap in the interim and should be the first thing 1.3.4/1.3.5 close.
non_blocking_findings.recommendation: When implementing 1.3.4 (recovery replay) and 1.3.5 (crash-injection), ensure OpenWriter's resume path is updated to validate (or at least tolerate/truncate) a torn tail on the resumed segment before allowing further Appends, not just on explicit recovery invocation -- otherwise a second untested code path (writer-level resume vs. explicit recovery) could diverge.

non_blocking_findings.summary: The commit message and writer.go's doc comments claim the durability idiom matches engine/catalog's 'WriteAt+Sync' pattern, but writer.go actually uses plain sequential file.Write+Sync (no WriteAt call). Functionally correct and arguably more appropriate for an append-only log, but the idiom-matching claim is imprecise.
non_blocking_findings.recommendation: Tighten the doc comment/commit-message wording in a follow-up pass to say 'Sync-before-return durability discipline' rather than implying a literal WriteAt call is shared with catalog.

non_blocking_findings.summary: docs/LLD/wal.md remains scaffold-level and was not updated by this diff to document the concrete 8-byte CRC32 record format, wal-<N>.log naming convention, or the deferred-torn-tail-validation decision -- consistent with the same LLD-staleness pattern already flagged for engine/btree in prior verification runs (032/034), so this is an inherited process gap, not newly introduced here, but wal.md is the only pre-existing design doc for this package and 1.3.2-1.3.5 will keep building on undocumented conventions if this isn't addressed soon.
non_blocking_findings.recommendation: Do an LLD-sync pass on docs/LLD/wal.md (record format, segment naming, rotation boundary semantics, deferred torn-tail scope) before or alongside 1.3.2, given this package's LLD is currently the most stale of any active package.

non_blocking_findings.summary: No dedicated test exercises the exact-boundary case (a record whose total size lands exactly at maxSegmentBytes, which the code correctly keeps in the current segment rather than rotating) or resuming with more than one pre-existing segment file.
non_blocking_findings.recommendation: Add a small follow-up test asserting a record landing exactly at the maxSegmentBytes boundary stays in the current segment (not rotated), and a resume test with >1 pre-existing segment file to confirm latestSegmentNum's sort/max logic holds beyond the single-segment case.

recommendation: Safe to mark subtask 1.3.1 verified and commit/push as-is. The core adversarial concern -- whether 'no record ever split across segments' is genuinely tested rather than merely asserted -- is confirmed to be real and strong: TestSegmentWriter forces >=3 segments, parses every segment via a genuine ReadSegment reader/parser (which hard-fails on any truncated header/payload or CRC mismatch, exactly what a split record would produce), and asserts full byte-for-byte, in-order equality across all 60 records reconstructed from all segments. The rotation-before-overflow boundary (strict '>' matching 'exceeds' semantics, not 'reaches') and the oversized-single-record hard-error path are both confirmed correct by direct code trace and passing tests, with no partial/corrupt bytes left on disk in the error case. fsync-before-return is genuine (Sync called and its error propagated before Append returns success). All tests pass under -race, and the full engine/... suite (btree, catalog, wal) shows zero regressions. The one real substantive concern -- OpenWriter's resume path not validating a resumed segment's torn tail -- is explicitly and reasonably deferred to 1.3.4/1.3.5 in the implementer's own documentation, not a silent landmine, but is flagged as medium-risk here so it is the first item those subtasks must close rather than something that could be quietly skipped. The remaining findings (imprecise WriteAt-idiom claim, stale wal.md LLD, missing exact-boundary/multi-segment-resume tests) are all low-risk, non-blocking follow-ups appropriate for a subsequent hardening pass, not reasons to withhold verification of this foundational subtask.
